title: Cornell Notes: Essentialism
description:
Cornell Notes is a proven note-taking system that helps you organize information, identify key concepts, and reflect on your learning. This method divides your notes into three sections: Cues/Questions for key terms and study prompts, Notes for main ideas and details, and Summary for synthesis and reflection. You'll apply this system to the video "Essentialism: Animated Book Summary" by Greg McKeown, creating notes that demonstrate your comprehension of the seven principles of essentialism and how they apply to achieving goals more effectively. You can also use this NoteBookLM to gain understanding.


Learning Objective:

  1. Web/Global IT: How can focusing on fewer, high-impact features lead to more successful web projects than trying to include everything?
  2. Finance: How can prioritizing essential financial goals and saying no to non-essential spending lead to greater financial success?

resources.title: Essentialism Video
resources.transcript: Eric was a software engineer with a really great idea for an app. And he had a dream to launch this amazing app that eventually gets millions of users where he becomes the next big tech guru in Silicon Valley. Let's take a look at how Eric spent the last couple of weeks. He wanted to improve his concentration so he could write better code for his app, but he notices that his house is a mess and decides to spend some time getting it cleaned up first. And he has so many different tech gadgets and trinkets without a great place to organize them. So, he decides to get some shelves to put everything on, but he can't find any shelves that perfectly fit in his office. So, he takes measurements, goes to Home Depot, buys wood and screws, and watches some YouTube videos on how to build his own shelves so he can neatly organize everything in his house. Eric's also got some friends who make pretty good money on YouTube, and he thinks that this could be another good income stream. So, he spends a few hours each day creating YouTube videos, teaching people how to code online. And then he spends about an hour every Monday and Thursday tutoring his nephew because he promised his brother he would. and he spent all of Sunday afternoon at a monthly family barbecue. Weeks go by like this and Eric feels stressed out because he's not seeing a lot of progress being made on his app. So, to try to make better progress, he just starts pounding energy drinks. He stops spending time at the gym or seeing his friends and he starts sleeping just 5 hours a night, which does actually let him spend more hours working on his project. But the deeper he gets into it, the more he starts realizing all the flaws with this idea and reasons for why it wouldn't be successful. but he's already spent so much time on it that he feels like he has to keep going and see it through. And then finally, after a year of working on it, he finally has a draft of his app ready, but he thinks it would be way more successful if it just had a few more features. So, he holds off on launching it so he can make it perfect before the big launch. According to Greg Mchuan's book, Essentialism, Eric's strategy for success is a failing one. And in some way or another, most people, even the really smart ones, fall into some of the same traps that Eric fell into and without even realizing it. The book recommends that by adopting the way of the essentialist, you can actually work way less than you probably are right now and still be way more likely to succeed. I'll take you through seven of the most important takeaways from the book, and then at the end, we'll revisit Eric and take a look at what his strategy should have looked like as a true essentialist. Now, without further ado, let's dive in. Principle number one, become extremely clear about your mission and priorities. This is the very first step to becoming an essentialist. None of the other principles in this book are going to work unless you first master this one. By becoming crystal clear on your mission, it lets you make priorities, which a lot of people actually don't know how to do. If you ask someone about their priorities, they'll start listing off a whole laundry list of things. But the very definition of a priority is when you have two things that come in conflict where you can only pick one. Which one are you picking? Greg tells the story of two companies. One of them had the following mission statement. We aim to be the leader in all our markets for the good of customers and shareholders. Does this sound similar to the mission statement of whatever company you work at? So many organizations have absurdly vague mission statements like these. Like what the does that statement even mean? It's not saying anything. just a bunch of word spaghetti. A different company in the book instead had the following mission statement. We believe that our first responsibility is to doctors, nurses, and patients to mothers, fathers, and everyone else who uses our product and services to meet their needs. Everything we do must be of the highest quality. This was the mission statement of pharmaceutical company Johnson and Johnson. It was actually used as a north star to guide the company in making difficult decisions. In 1982, seven people died after taking Tylenol, a drug made by Johnson and Johnson. It was eventually discovered that those pills were poisoned with cyanide. So, what did Johnson and Johnson do? They immediately withdrew every single Tylenol bottle from every store it was distributed to. This was not an easy pill to swallow, pun intended, because the move cost the company's shareholders $und00 million. But it was an easy decision to make because the company's mission statement and priorities were clear. Customers first, shareholders last. The whole point of making priorities isn't to help you make easy decisions. Because if the decision was easy to make, you wouldn't need defined priorities in the first place. Clearly defined priorities are there precisely to help you make the hard decisions. In your own life, you probably have your own dreams and goals and stuff you want to accomplish, but you probably also have a bunch of other less important stuff chip away at your time every day. The first step in correcting this is becoming extremely clear about what your mission and priorities are. Principle two, being busy does not equal being productive. This is an extremely common mind trap that a lot of smart people in particular fall into, and it typically goes something like this. Thinking about starting a big important project gives you a lot of anxiety like studying for your final exams or doing that big project at work. But you also don't want to feel unproductive by like watching TV or something. So what you might do is spend some time on tasks that seem productive but actually aren't getting you any closer to your goals at all, like cleaning up your email inbox or pulling the weeds out of your lawn or reorganizing your closet. This lets your brain feel like you're spending your time on worthwhile things, but it's a trap because in reality, you're not getting any closer to the important goals in your life. Now, I don't mean you should never do things like reorganize your closet or pull weeds, but you should be extremely careful to limit how much time you spend on shallow tasks like that to no more than an hour a day and only after you've made real progress toward your actual goals. Otherwise, stuff like that is just going to fill up the majority of your day. and a year will go by and you'll be in the same place making the exact same New Year's resolution that you made last year since you won't be any closer to your goals. Principle three, ignore good opportunities so you can go 100% allin on the great opportunities. There's literally over a thousand different ways to make a lot of money and at least a hundred of those are great ways to make a lot of money, but if you try to do all of them, you're going to end up not making any money. Imagine you broke your knee and you needed a doctor to perform a complex, delicate surgery to fix it. So you meet with two doctors. One of them has made an entire specialty focusing on complex knee repair and has performed over a 100 surgeries identical to yours in the last year. The other doctor is not only more of a generalist focusing on lots of different types of surgeries, but also did a career change a few years back where he actually used to be an accountant and still spends half his time running a tax preparation business on the side. Which doctor would you rather have performing your surgery? Probably the one who has gone allin on becoming an excellent specialized knee surgeon. Maybe this isn't the most realistic example, but you get the point. If you want to be truly great at something, you have to say no to being good at a bunch of different things. Warren Buffett, one of the world's most successful investors and one of the richest people in the world, owes 90% of his entire fortune to just 10 investments. Some ignorant people like to rag on Buffett for missing amazing investment opportunities like Tesla and Amazon in the mid210s. But Buffett doesn't care. Again, there's hundreds of stocks that could have made him extremely rich. But it doesn't matter because not only is it impossible to accurately predict them all, it's also unnecessary and would just be a huge waste of time and energy because he can instead just focus on the few opportunities that he's extremely confident in and go all in on those. The book suggests a very quick and easy test you can use to decide whether you should spend your time on a particular opportunity. You should ask yourself, is this opportunity a hell yes? If it's not an absolute energetic, extremely excited hell yes, then it should be a clear no. Find the few opportunities that are hell yeses and focus all your attention on those. Principle four, learn to say no a lot more frequently than you say yes. This is another extremely common trap that people fall into. So many of us are just massive people pleasers, especially when it comes to our family or friends or people we work with. But the book says basically to politeness and start making a good habit out of telling people no. Other people are always going to take as much time away from you as you allow them. A technique you can use to help you here is this. Imagine people aren't asking you for your time, but for your 16-year-old son's time, and your son is already working 60 hours a week at two different jobs. You probably wouldn't feel as bad telling people in 90% of those cases, "No, sorry, my son just doesn't have time for that right now." Right? Well, whatever your mission or goal or dream is, you need to treat that like it's your overworked 16-year-old son living in a world where child labor laws don't exist and stop pedaling them around town for everyone else's benefit. And even though I'm a firm believer that no is a complete sentence, you also don't have to be a dick about it if you don't want to. So instead of committing the time and energy that you don't have by saying yes, you can follow up your polite yet firm no with a proposal for an alternative that could still help out the friend or family member who's asking you for help. So for example, if they ask you if you can drive them somewhere, you can just say, "No, sorry. I don't have time for that right now, but here's the keys to my car. You're welcome to take it." Or if your boss asks you to take on more work at your day job, you can say, "Absolutely. I'd love to help out with this. Um, currently my day is already filled up with tasks X, Y, and Z. Which one of those should I deprioritize to make a room for this new task?" Principle five, cut your losses before they drag you down further. Let's do a short quiz. Let's say you spent $100 on tickets to go skiing for a weekend in Michigan in a couple months. But then a few weeks later, you buy tickets to go skiing in Colorado for 50 bucks, and you totally forgot that those tickets fall on the same weekend as your Michigan ski trip. And even though your Michigan tickets cost $100, you think you'd actually have way more fun skiing in Colorado. Most people are literally hardwired to still choose going to Michigan that weekend because they don't like the feeling of wasting that more expensive ticket. But in reality, this would be irrational because the money on the tickets is already spent and non-refundable. and you'll be spending that weekend having less fun than you would somewhere else. This is called a sunk cost fallacy and there's a ton of studies that prove how impactful this is. It's the same reason why people overeat at buffets until they can't breathe or why people who already lost a ton of money at the casino throw even more money at it to try and recoup their losses. The book argues that you have to consciously fight back against this emotional tendency. Only when you admit and accept a failure that's already happened can you begin the path to success. Non-essentialists think, "I've already thrown so much time and money at this, I have to keep going so that it wasn't all for nothing." While essentialists have the mindset of, "If I cut my losses right now, what could I accomplish with the remaining in time and money that I'd be saving?" Principle six, rest and recovery is not optional. It's essential. Sleep is the most important thing you can do for your success. Your number one priority every single day should be to get a full night of sleep. The that do job bragging about how they only suffer 5 hours a night like it's some badge of honor are ignorant and digging themselves in early grave. I spent a decade working in banking and consulting. And I'm telling you, there's no shortage of these idiots. Remember our second principle that being busy does not equal being productive. Imagine that what's separating you from your goals and dreams is a maze. When you're working on the right things, you're taking the most efficient path through that maze. When you're working on the wrong things, you end up wasting time going down the wrong paths in a maze. The main factor that's going to determine whether you're spending time on the right things or the wrong things is your ideas. What you mentally decide to spend your time on. And if you want to have good ideas, you have to be well-rested. You have to be healthy and you have to spend at least some time doing things that are fun and enjoyable so that your mind can relax. That's why there's so many cases of people coming up with those eureka moment ideas randomly in the shower or while taking a walk with their dog. Your subconscious mind that generates creative ideas works its best in states of joy and relaxation. Principle seven, iterate. Don't get stuck in analysis paralysis. Perfectionism is not a good thing. There's almost nothing on this planet that's worth doing 100% perfectly. Now, I'm not saying you should do a bad job or not care about taking pride in your work, but you need to understand that once whatever task you're working on reaches the level of good enough, you're going to see rapidly diminishing returns the more time you spend on it. And you're spending an enormous opportunity cost on the time that you could have spent moving on to the next task. In business, you have the idea of minimum viable product. What is the simplest possible product that will be useful to the intended audience? The book suggests to adopt this approach in everything that you do. Focus on the fastest and simplest path to a realistic complete outcome. Then launch it, get feedback, and iterate further after that. Don't spend years turnurning on getting every possible tiny detail 100% perfectly right before ever launching anything. This paralysis by overanalysis ends up being the graveyard for the ideas and business ventures of a lot of perfectionists. Let's make Eric an essentialist. Remember Eric from the start of the video? Let's apply all seven of the principles we learned from this book to turn him into an essentialist and correct what he should have done if he wanted to achieve his goals. The very first thing he should have done is followed principle one and written a clearly defined mission statement for his goals and priorities. Something along the lines of my goal is to design and launch an app that within the next 12 months gets 1 million active users. When he realizes that his house being a mess is too much of a distraction for him, he should have applied principle two and made a plan for the fastest possible way to get his house organized. It shouldn't be how can I make my house as organized as humanly possible. It should be what is the fastest and simplest level of organization that would be enough so that I'm no longer distracted. He definitely should not have spent time building custom shelves. And by the way, if you're wondering where I got this idea for building custom shelves, it's for myself. I I did this. Here's the shelves that I built. And yes, the shelves are nice, but it was a stupid activity, and it cost me a month of my time. When Eric gets the idea from his friends to start a YouTube channel to make extra money, he should have applied principle three and realized, "Yes, this may indeed be a good idea. But if I had to choose between a YouTube channel and launching this app, I would still choose the app. The YouTube channel would take my time away from the app. So, I'm going to say no to the YouTube channel idea." When his brother asks him to spend a couple hours a week tutoring his nephew in programming, he should have applied principle 4 and said, "Let me think about it and get back to you." And then found other good resources like good textbooks, YouTube channels, or other established tutors that he can recommend instead while politely declining because he unfortunately just doesn't have the time at the moment. By providing a carefully made list of other helpful resources, it would still show his brother or nephew that he genuinely does care and there should be no bad blood between them. When Eric started realizing fundamental flaws in his app idea, he should have applied principle 5 and taken some time to do some creative and critical thinking on how to best pivot his idea to something else. Even though he might feel like the last few months of effort would have gone to waste, it's better to just take it as a learning experience instead of wasting even more months on a doomed idea. When he started giving up on sleep, nutrition, exercise, or any form of recreation, he should have realized that he was violating principle six. The time he might save by skipping doing all those things will be completely dwarfed by the efficiency, quality, and creative thinking he would lose by putting himself in such an unhealthy state. And when his app finally did reach a state of minimum viable product, he should have applied principle 7 and at least launched to a small beta testing group to get feedback from actual users that could help generate ideas that he never would have thought of in a silo. Don't be like Eric and start following each of these seven principles. It definitely won't be easy, but it is essential. All right, really quick. If you like these concepts and you want to refer back to them, I made this really colorful one-page mind map that summarizes all the key points from this video. If you click the link in the description, I'll email it to you completely for free. And if you already subscribe to my newsletter, this mind map should already be sitting in your inbox. And if you want to see more videos like this, please hit like and subscribe. Thanks again for watching. Good luck in your adventure and I'll see you in the next
resources.transcript_summary:
This video is a tutorial on how to adopt essentialism to achieve success. The speaker uses a fictional character named Eric to illustrate the common pitfalls people fall into when trying to achieve their goals. Eric, a software engineer, is overwhelmed with various tasks such as cleaning his house, building shelves, creating YouTube videos, tutoring his nephew, and attending family gatherings, which all take time away from his main goal of developing an app.

The speaker then introduces seven principles from Greg Mchuan's book, Essentialism. The first principle is to have a clear mission and set of priorities. The second principle emphasizes that being busy doesn't equate to being productive. The third principle advises ignoring good opportunities to focus on the great ones. The fourth principle suggests saying "no" more often to prioritize your goals. The fifth principle encourages cutting losses before they drag you down further. The sixth principle highlights the importance of rest and recovery. The seventh principle recommends avoiding perfectionism and encourages launching a 'minimum viable product' and iterating on it based on feedback.

Applying these principles to Eric's situation, the speaker suggests that Eric should have prioritized his app development, declined non-essential tasks, pivoted his app idea when he noticed flaws, maintained a healthy lifestyle, and launched a basic version of his app for feedback. The speaker concludes by emphasizing that adopting these principles is essential for success.
resources.transcript_prompt: Review the transcript, then summarize it for high school students. Start with; This video is a tutorial on how to ......

sections.title: Project Setup
sections.description: Complete the technical requirements to set up your Cornell Notes document. This includes verifying your file structure, adding header information, and renaming your file correctly. These steps ensure GradeFlow can evaluate your work accurately and your teacher has the context needed to assess your notes.

objectives.title: File Check
objectives.description:
This task makes sure your file contains the proper elements, and verifies you've know how to use the Evaluate button, task resources, and task hints. Watch the task video, and do not continue until you have earned all points.


If you can not pass this task with all points, please follow the recovery steps in this recovery video.

objectives.title: Header Section
objectives.description:
This information is required for your teacher, and for GradeFlow to evaluate your assignment within the context of the class, topic, and learning objective. Incorrect or incomplete information will negatively impact grading.


  1. Specific Topic: Enter the main subject of your notes (e.g., "Causes of the Civil War," not "History").
  2. Source Title / URL: Enter the title of the video, article, or textbook chapter, and hyperlink to its URL if available.
  3. If no URL, add a hyperlink to "https://app.gradeflow.net".
  4. Student Name: Enter your first and last name.
  5. Class Name: Enter the name of your class (e.g., "US History Period 3").
  6. Learning Objective: Enter the correct Learning Objective listed below.
  7. Web/Global IT: How can focusing on fewer, high-impact features lead to more successful web projects than trying to include everything?
  8. Finance: How can prioritizing essential financial goals and saying no to non-essential spending lead to greater financial success?

objectives.title: File Name
objectives.description:
Rename your file using this naming convention:

Last, First | Assignment Name | Period


Example:

Benrud, Todd | Cornell Notes | Period #5

sections.title: NOTES Section
sections.description: Record information from the Essentialism video in an organized format. Each row represents one of the key principles from the video. In the Cues/Questions column, identify vocabulary related to productivity, priorities, and decision-making, and create questions that will help you study. In the Notes column, capture the main ideas, supporting examples (like Eric's story), and practical applications using your own words.

objectives.title: Row #1 Notes
objectives.description:
Row 1: Begin with the first main idea or concept from your source. Capture the most important point introduced at the start of the audio, video, or material.

  1. Cues/Questions: Write key terms, vocabulary, and questions that will help you study later.
  2. Notes: Record main ideas, details, definitions, and examples in your own words.


*GradeFlow evaluates your work for completion. Your teacher grade for quality when you turn in your assignment.

objectives.title: Row #2 Notes
objectives.description:
Row 2: Begin with the first main idea or concept from your source. Capture the most important point introduced at the start of the audio, video, or material.

  1. Cues/Questions: Write key terms, vocabulary, and questions that will help you study later.
  2. Notes: Record main ideas, details, definitions, and examples in your own words.


*GradeFlow evaluates your work for completion. Your teacher grade for quality when you turn in your assignment.

objectives.title: Row #3 Notes
objectives.description:
Row 3: Begin with the first main idea or concept from your source. Capture the most important point introduced at the start of the audio, video, or material.

  1. Cues/Questions: Write key terms, vocabulary, and questions that will help you study later.
  2. Notes: Record main ideas, details, definitions, and examples in your own words.


*GradeFlow evaluates your work for completion. Your teacher grade for quality when you turn in your assignment.

sections.title: SUMMARY Section
sections.description: Synthesize what you've learned by writing a brief summary that captures the main ideas from all seven principles. Connect essentialism concepts to your own life, goals, or decision-making, and identify questions that remain unanswered or areas you want to explore further.

objectives.title: SUMMARY Writing
objectives.description:
GREEN Outlined area.

In your own words, summarize the main idea in 2-3 sentences. Explain how this information connects to what you already know and identify at least once question you have.


*GradeFlow evaluates your work for completion. Your teacher grade for quality when you turn in your assignment.

sections.title: Teacher Graded
sections.description: Your teacher will evaluate the overall quality of your Cornell Notes, including how well your notes align with the learning objective, the depth of your understanding of Essentialism principles, and the thoughtfulness of your questions and summary.

resources.title: AI Feedback / Scoring
resources.transcript: All right. So, I have submitted my um assignment to the teacher and I've waited about maybe 90 seconds, two minutes, and checked back on it. And um I can see now that I've got scores. So, I've got 132 out of 150. And these teacher graded tasks, they've been pregraded by AI. Now, your teachers are going to use these to inform their final grading, but you can use this also to help. So over on the left hand side I can see that um I've got the task and then down below I got the feedback. Now this feedback is generated by AI and so you can read through it and it's going to tell you what you did well, what you need to work on, um why you didn't get all of the points and that's going to be true for each of the tasks where you did not get all of the points. So read that feedback and then use it to make adjustments to um what you put in this area. Now you'll notice over here on the completed one, I have a teacher graded one that's marked complete the notes section. And that's because I got all of the points. And if I read this feedback, it's going to be, hey, you did a great job. Everything was perfect. Right? So, um the AI, it's going to grade you like a teacher would. It's not going to always give you the points just because you gave it a good shot. it's going to be subjective and so you can look at that feedback um use your you know critical thinking uh use your knowledge of the work um you can unsubmit you can make updates and then you can go back and resubmit and wait a few minutes get the AI feedback and you can do that loop um a few times before you turn it
resources.transcript_summary: This video is a tutorial on how to use AI-graded assignments. The speaker describes how, after submitting an assignment, they received a score and feedback from the AI grading system. This feedback outlines what was done well and what needs improvement, providing guidance for areas to focus on. The AI system grades similarly to a human teacher and doesn't award points simply for trying. The speaker suggests using this feedback to revise and resubmit the assignment, repeating this process as necessary to improve the score. The AI feedback serves as a tool for self-improvement and aids in the final grading by the teacher.
resources.transcript_prompt: Review the transcript, then summarize it for high school students. Start with; This video is a tutorial on how to ......

objectives.title: General Review
objectives.description:
Your notes will be evaluated for completeness and alignment with the learning objective.

  1. The NOTES column should include key vocabulary (such as priorities, sunk cost fallacy, minimum viable product), main concepts from the seven principles, and supporting details relevant to essentialism.
  2. Your CUES/QUESTIONS should reflect the main ideas from your notes and demonstrate thinking beyond simple recall, such as asking how a principle might apply to real situations.
  3. The SUMMARY section should accurately capture the main ideas of essentialism in 2-3 sentences, include a connection to prior knowledge or personal experience, and identify at least one remaining question.
  4. All sections must be internally consistent and align with the stated Learning Objective.

data.user_prompt:
`Grading Criteria`

1) Confirm the NOTES column includes key vocabulary (priorities, mission statement, sunk cost fallacy, minimum viable product, iterate), main concepts from the seven essentialism principles, and supporting details relevant to the stated Topic and Learning Objective. The NOTES column must demonstrate understanding beyond copying source material.

2) Confirm the CUES/QUESTIONS column contains terms and questions that reflect main ideas from the NOTES column. Questions should demonstrate engagement beyond surface recall, such as application or analysis questions.

3) Confirm the SUMMARY section accurately reflects main ideas in 2-3 sentences, includes a connection to prior knowledge, and identifies at least one remaining question.

4) Verify that content across all sections is internally consistent and aligns with the stated Learning Objective. The Learning Objective connects essentialism principles to a specific domain (such as web development or finance), so notes and summary should demonstrate application to that context. If alignment cannot be verified due to vague or missing information, return a 0 for this criterion.

objectives.title: NOTES Section Review
objectives.description: Your notes will be evaluated for originality and depth. Notes should be written in your own words rather than copied from the video, demonstrating that you understand the material well enough to paraphrase it. Each row should include specific details, examples (such as Eric's shelving project or Warren Buffett's investment strategy), or explanations that support the main idea. Your CUES/QUESTIONS should demonstrate analytical thinking by requiring explanation or analysis rather than one-word answers, and cues should identify key vocabulary and important concepts related to essentialism.

data.user_prompt:
CONTEXT GATHERING:
Locate the hyperlinked text in the document's "Sub Title" field (located directly below the main title). Extract the URL from this hyperlink. If the URL is anything OTHER than "https://app.gradeflow.net" or "app.gradeflow.net", attempt to fetch and review the source content to understand what the student was taking notes on. If the fetch fails, the page is inaccessible, or the URL is "app.gradeflow.net", proceed with evaluation using only the document content.

`Grading Criteria`
1) Confirm notes are written in the student's own words rather than copied directly from the video transcript. Notes should paraphrase concepts and explain ideas in a way that demonstrates understanding. Each row should include specific details, examples (such as Eric's story, Warren Buffett's investments, or Johnson & Johnson's mission statement), or explanations that support the main idea.

2) Confirm CUES/QUESTIONS demonstrate thinking beyond simple recall. Questions should require explanation or analysis rather than one-word answers (for example, "How could saying no more often help me reach my goals?" rather than "What is principle 4?"). Cues should identify key vocabulary and important concepts related to essentialism and productivity.

3) Confirm at least two Note rows explicitly connects an essentialism principle to the domain context specified in the Learning Objective.

objectives.title: SUMMARY Section Review
objectives.description: Your summary will be evaluated for synthesis and reflection. The summary should capture the main ideas from all note rows in 2-3 complete sentences, synthesizing the seven principles rather than focusing on just one. Your connection to prior knowledge should be specific, explaining how essentialism concepts relate to your own goals, experiences, or decision-making. Your remaining question should demonstrate genuine intellectual curiosity by identifying a meaningful gap in understanding or exploring how you might apply essentialism in your life.

data.user_prompt:
`Grading Criteria`
1) Confirm the summary captures the main ideas from the notes in 2-5 complete sentences. The summary should synthesize information across all rows (covering multiple essentialism principles) rather than focusing on only one detail.

2) Confirm the connection to prior knowledge is specific and the remaining question demonstrates genuine curiosity. The student should explain how essentialism concepts relate to their own goals, experiences, or decision-making. Their question should identify a meaningful gap in understanding or seek to explore how they might apply these principles in their life.

3) Confirm the remaining question demonstrates genuine curiosity and relates to applying essentialism within the domain context of the Learning Objective. The question should identify a meaningful gap in understanding or explore practical application rather than asking something already answered in the video.
